title: Near-duplicate detection across a billion-document corpus
goal: Design and tune a near-duplicate detection algorithm that scales to a billion documents within a fixed memory and throughput budget.

branches.reason: root line: frame the problem and converge on a final design
explore exact-hashing as the cheap baseline
explore shingling + MinHash + LSH for fuzzy recall
parallel explore: SimHash + Hamming-distance banding, first pass
memory constraint forces a decision between the two fuzzy approaches
revisit the stashed SimHash idea once memory bites
converge on a hybrid design with concrete parameters

units.full: I need to detect near-duplicate documents across a corpus that is heading toward a billion documents. The goal is not byte-identical copies but documents that are mostly the same: boilerplate-wrapped reposts, lightly edited articles, templated spam. So this is a similarity problem, not an equality problem, and the hard part is that the corpus is enormous, so I cannot afford an O(n^2) pairwise comparison and I cannot hold rich per-document state in RAM. Let me frame three candidate families up front: exact hashing as a cheap baseline, shingling with MinHash plus LSH for tunable fuzzy recall, and SimHash with Hamming-distance banding for a compact fingerprint. I will weigh accuracy against memory and throughput.
units.thought: Frame near-duplicate detection at billion-document scale as a similarity problem under memory and throughput limits; candidates are exact hashing, minhash plus lsh, and simhash.

units.full: Before choosing, I should pin the actual constraints, because they decide everything. Throughput target is roughly fifty thousand documents per second on ingest, single rack, so per-document work has to stay in the low microseconds. Memory budget is the real squeeze: a few hundred gigabytes total for the whole index, which at a billion documents means only a couple hundred bytes per document, including the inverted structures used for candidate lookup. Recall matters more than precision here because a cheap exact verification step can clean up false positives downstream, but a missed near-duplicate is gone forever. So I will optimize for high recall at fixed memory, then bolt on verification.
units.thought: Pin constraints: ~50k docs/sec ingest, a few hundred bytes per document of memory, and prioritize recall since a cheap exact verify can prune false positives later.

units.full: There are really two open sub-questions I can chase in parallel. One: can a cheap exact-hashing baseline catch enough of the duplicates to be worth keeping as a first stage? Two: which fuzzy method, MinHash plus LSH or SimHash, gives me the best recall-per-byte under that brutal memory budget? I will spin up an explore branch for the exact baseline to set a floor, and separately explore the two fuzzy candidates against each other. I will keep the fuzzy comparison honest by holding the same target: detect documents above roughly eighty percent Jaccard or content similarity, with recall above ninety percent.
units.thought: Split into parallel sub-questions: an exact-hashing baseline for a floor, and a head-to-head between minhash-plus-lsh and simhash for best recall-per-byte at ~80 percent similarity.

units.full: Take the exact-hashing baseline first. I hash the full normalized document with a 64-bit hash and bucket on equality. This is trivially cheap, one hash per document, constant memory per entry, and it perfectly catches byte-identical or whitespace-normalized copies. But it is brittle: a single inserted word, a changed timestamp, a reordered paragraph, and the hash diverges completely, so recall on genuine near-duplicates collapses. I could shingle and exact-hash each shingle, but then I am storing many hashes per document and I have wandered into the fuzzy territory anyway. So exact hashing is a useful exact-dedup pre-filter and a verification primitive, but it cannot be the near-duplicate detector. It sets the floor.
units.thought: Exact full-document hashing is cheap and perfect for identical copies but brittle to any edit, so it serves as an exact-dedup pre-filter and verification primitive, not the near-duplicate detector.

units.full: Now the MinHash plus LSH branch. The recipe: tokenize each document into k-shingles, say k equals five word-grams, then compute a MinHash signature of length r by taking r independent min-hashes over the shingle set. Two documents' signatures agree on a given position with probability equal to their Jaccard similarity, so the fraction of agreeing positions estimates Jaccard directly. Then LSH bands the signature into b bands of rows each; documents colliding in any band become candidates. The classic S-curve threshold sits near the b-and-rows tradeoff, so I tune the bands so the probability of becoming a candidate rises sharply right around eighty percent similarity. This is the textbook high-recall fuzzy method.
units.thought: MinHash over k-shingles estimates Jaccard from signature agreement, and LSH bands the signature so collision probability rises sharply near the 80 percent threshold, giving tunable high recall.

units.full: I want concrete MinHash parameters. With a signature of around 128 min-hashes I get a Jaccard estimate with standard error near one over the square root of 128, roughly nine percent, which is acceptable around the eighty percent band. For LSH, the candidate probability for a pair at similarity s is one minus one minus s-to-the-rows, all raised to the bands. Picking, say, sixteen bands of eight rows each over the 128 hashes puts the S-curve knee near zero-point-eight, which is what I want. Per-document cost is 128 hash evaluations plus sixteen band-hash lookups, well within the microsecond budget at fifty thousand documents per second across a few cores. The recall and precision look strong on paper.
units.thought: Concrete minhash params: 128 hashes give about 9 percent Jaccard error, and 16 bands of 8 rows place the lsh S-curve knee near 0.8, with per-document cost inside the microsecond budget.

units.full: In parallel I will sketch the SimHash branch so I can compare fairly rather than anchoring on MinHash. SimHash projects the weighted token set of a document into a single fixed-width fingerprint, say 64 bits: each feature votes plus or minus on each bit by its own hash, and the sign of the accumulated weight per bit gives the final bit. The crucial property is that the Hamming distance between two SimHash fingerprints is small exactly when the documents are similar, so near-duplicate detection becomes a near-neighbor search in Hamming space. To find candidates without scanning everything, I band the 64 bits into blocks and index permutations so that any pair within k bits collides in at least one block. Compact and elegant; the question is recall.
units.thought: SimHash maps each document to a single 64-bit fingerprint whose Hamming distance tracks similarity, and banded bit-blocks with permuted indexes find low-Hamming candidates without a full scan.

units.full: Let me verify the MinHash plus LSH complexity and memory honestly, because the band hashes are not free. Ingest is order n times signature-length for hashing, so linear in the corpus, good. The killer is memory: an LSH index needs, per document, the band keys plus a posting pointer for candidate retrieval. With sixteen bands I am storing sixteen band-hash entries per document in inverted lists, plus the 128-hash signature itself if I want to re-estimate Jaccard at verification time. Even at four bytes a band key that is sixty-four bytes of band index per document, and the signature is another 128 bytes at one byte per min-hash. That is around two hundred bytes per document just for MinHash. At a billion documents that is two hundred gigabytes, right at the edge of my budget.
units.thought: Verifying minhash plus lsh memory: about 64 bytes of band index plus a 128-byte signature per document, roughly 200 bytes each, which at a billion documents lands right at the memory budget edge.

units.full: I should sanity-check the recall and precision of the LSH design before I commit, not just the memory. For a pair at exactly the eighty percent target with sixteen bands of eight rows, the candidate probability is one minus one minus zero-point-eight-to-the-eighth, raised to the sixteenth. Zero-point-eight to the eighth is about zero-point-one-seven, so per-band collision is seventeen percent, and the chance of colliding in none of sixteen bands is zero-point-eight-three to the sixteenth, roughly zero-point-zero-five, giving about ninety-five percent recall at the threshold. Below threshold the curve falls off steeply, so precision is decent and the exact-hash verify stage handles the rest. The numbers hold; the design is sound on accuracy.
units.thought: Recall check: at 80 percent similarity, 16 bands of 8 rows give about 95 percent candidate recall with a steep S-curve, so precision is acceptable and the exact verify stage cleans the remainder.

units.full: Stepping back to the main line to take stock of where the two fuzzy branches stand. MinHash plus LSH is accuracy-strong and tunable, with verified recall near ninety-five percent, but it is memory-hungry at roughly two hundred bytes per document, sitting right on the budget ceiling with no headroom for growth. SimHash is far more compact, a single sixty-four-bit fingerprint plus a small permuted index, but I have not yet verified its recall, and SimHash is known to be weaker than MinHash at capturing set overlap because it compresses everything into one fingerprint. So the real tension is exactly accuracy versus memory, which is the tradeoff I flagged at the start. I need to push on the memory number harder.
units.thought: Taking stock: minhash plus lsh wins on verified recall but sits at the memory ceiling, while simhash is far more compact but unverified and weaker at set overlap, so it is accuracy versus memory.

units.full: A constraint just hardened that changes the calculus. The platform team confirmed the index has to share the box with the serving layer and live retrieval, so my real memory budget for the dedup index is more like one hundred gigabytes, not the few hundred I assumed. That is a hard ceiling. At a billion documents that is one hundred bytes per document, total, for everything: fingerprints and the candidate-lookup structure combined. MinHash plus LSH at two hundred bytes per document is now flatly over budget by two times, no amount of tuning the bands gets me there without gutting recall. The memory constraint just bit, exactly as I worried. This forces a decision and probably forces me back to the compact fingerprint.
units.thought: The memory budget hardened to 100 GB, so 100 bytes per document total; minhash plus lsh at 200 bytes is now two times over budget, forcing a decision back toward a compact fingerprint.

units.full: Given the hardened one-hundred-byte-per-document ceiling, I have to make the call. I could try to shrink MinHash: drop to sixty-four min-hashes and fewer bands, but the Jaccard standard error climbs past twelve percent and the S-curve gets sloppy right where I need it sharpest, so recall at the threshold sags below ninety percent. That trades away the one thing MinHash was winning on. The honest read is that MinHash plus LSH cannot fit the new budget without losing its accuracy edge, so it stops dominating. That means the compact-fingerprint approach I sketched earlier and then set aside is suddenly the front-runner. I should revisit SimHash seriously now rather than mutilate MinHash.
units.thought: Decision: shrinking minhash to fit 100 bytes wrecks its recall edge, so it no longer dominates; the compact simhash fingerprint I stashed earlier becomes the front-runner and deserves a serious revisit.

units.full: Revisiting SimHash properly now that memory is the binding constraint. Its whole appeal is density: one sixty-four-bit fingerprint is eight bytes per document, two orders of magnitude under the MinHash signature. For candidate retrieval I use the permuted-table trick: to find all fingerprints within Hamming distance k of a query, I build a few tables, each a different rotation of the sixty-four bits, sorted so that a high-order prefix match isolates a small block to scan. With four tables covering the bit positions, any pair within three bits is guaranteed to share a prefix in at least one table. Four sorted tables of eight-byte keys is about thirty-two bytes per document of index, plus the eight-byte fingerprint. Forty bytes total, comfortably inside one hundred.
units.thought: Revisited simhash fits the budget: an 8-byte fingerprint plus four permuted sorted tables for Hamming-k retrieval is about 40 bytes per document, well under the 100-byte ceiling.

units.full: I have to verify SimHash recall, since that was the open worry when I first stashed it. The mapping from content similarity to Hamming distance is probabilistic: for sixty-four-bit fingerprints, two documents at eighty percent feature similarity differ on roughly twenty percent of the bits in expectation, about thirteen bits, which is well above any small k I would band on. That is the catch: a strict Hamming threshold of three bits only catches very strong near-duplicates, so plain SimHash recall at the eighty percent level is poor, maybe sixty percent. So my earlier instinct that SimHash is weaker on set overlap was right. SimHash alone does not clear the ninety percent recall bar. I cannot just swap it in; I need to fix the recall gap.
units.thought: Verifying simhash recall confirms the old worry: at 80 percent similarity fingerprints differ by about 13 bits, so a strict 3-bit Hamming band recalls only around 60 percent; simhash alone misses the recall bar.

units.full: Let me compute whether widening the Hamming band rescues SimHash recall without blowing up candidates. If I accept matches up to k equals six bits instead of three, I capture more of that thirteen-bit spread and recall climbs, but the number of bit-combinations within k grows like sixty-four-choose-k, and worse, the permuted-table scheme needs more tables to guarantee that any within-k pair shares a prefix: roughly k plus one tables for a sixty-four-bit key split into blocks. At k equals six that is around seven tables, so the index balloons from forty bytes toward seventy or eighty bytes per document, and each query scans more blocks, hurting throughput. So widening the band trades memory and speed straight back for the recall I lost. There is no free lunch in pure SimHash; the recall-memory frontier is just shaped differently than MinHash.
units.thought: Computing the widen-the-band fix: raising Hamming k to 6 lifts recall but needs about 7 permuted tables and 70 to 80 bytes per document plus more scanning, trading memory and throughput straight back for recall.

units.full: Tangential thought while I have the bit-arithmetic loaded: I should remember to set the random seed for the SimHash feature hash family and the MinHash hash family explicitly in the config, because a non-deterministic build would make the dedup results irreproducible across index rebuilds and break any A/B comparison I run later. Also worth a note to log the exact library version of the hashing primitive, since a silent upgrade that changes the hash would silently invalidate every fingerprint in the live index. Not part of the algorithm proper, but it would be an embarrassing operational bug to discover after a rebuild. Back to the recall problem.
units.thought: Operational aside: pin the random seed and log the hash library version so the simhash and minhash fingerprints stay reproducible across index rebuilds and A/B runs.

units.full: Here is the unlocking idea: I do not have to choose SimHash purely for accuracy and MinHash purely for memory; I can use SimHash as a cheap, compact first-stage candidate generator and accept its imperfect recall there, then verify survivors more precisely. But verification needs the richer signal that only something MinHash-like provides, and I just said I cannot afford a full MinHash signature per document. So the hypothesis is a two-tier design: a dense SimHash band that is generous on recall by using a moderate Hamming k, feeding a cheap exact-overlap verify on the small candidate set, and only computing an expensive MinHash-style estimate on demand for the survivors, never stored at rest. That keeps the at-rest memory dominated by the eight-byte fingerprint plus a modest index.
units.thought: Hypothesis: a two-tier design where a generous simhash band generates candidates and a cheap on-demand exact or minhash-style verify prunes them, keeping only the 8-byte fingerprint plus a modest index at rest.

units.full: I need to verify the recall of the two-tier scheme, because a first stage that misses a true duplicate can never be recovered by a verify stage; verification only removes, it never adds. So the SimHash band must itself recall above ninety percent. From the bit math, getting recall above ninety at eighty percent similarity needs a Hamming k around eight or nine bits, which is too many for the permuted-table trick to stay compact. The fix is to widen the fingerprint instead of the band: use a 128-bit SimHash, so the proportional bit-spread is the same fraction but absolute thresholds give finer control, and band it so any within-fraction pair collides. A 128-bit fingerprint is sixteen bytes, still tiny, and lets me hit recall while keeping k per block small.
units.thought: Verifying the two-tier recall: the simhash first stage must itself exceed 90 percent recall, so widen to a 128-bit fingerprint for finer banding control rather than enlarging the Hamming band on 64 bits.

units.full: Let me size the 128-bit SimHash banding concretely. Split the 128 bits into, say, four blocks of thirty-two bits. For two documents within Hamming distance k overall, the pigeonhole principle says at least one block differs by at most k over four bits, so if I want to catch pairs within k equals sixteen overall I need each block to tolerate four-bit differences in its block, which I get by indexing each block's prefix and scanning the small neighborhood. Four block-tables of sixteen-byte keys is sixty-four bytes of index, plus the sixteen-byte fingerprint, eighty bytes per document. That fits under one hundred with a little headroom. Recall at eighty percent similarity with this band lands around ninety-two percent by the same S-curve reasoning, clearing the bar.
units.thought: Computing the 128-bit band: four 32-bit blocks let pigeonhole catch within-16-bit pairs, costing about 80 bytes per document and giving roughly 92 percent recall, which fits the budget and clears the bar.

units.full: I should verify the candidate volume, because banding recall is worthless if it floods the verify stage with junk. With four blocks each indexing a thirty-two-bit prefix, the expected number of random fingerprints sharing a block prefix at a billion documents is one billion over two-to-the-thirty-two, roughly a quarter of a document per prefix on average, so spurious collisions are rare and the candidate set per query stays in the low tens once true near-duplicates and chance prefix matches are summed across four tables. That keeps the verify stage cheap. The verify itself is a recomputed token-overlap or a Hamming recheck at full 128-bit width, microseconds each over a handful of candidates. Throughput holds at the fifty-thousand-per-second target. The design closes.
units.thought: Verifying candidate volume: four 32-bit prefix blocks yield only low-tens of candidates per query at a billion docs, so the verify stage stays cheap and throughput holds at 50k per second.

units.full: Bringing the branches back to the main line to decide. Exact hashing stays, but only as a zero-cost pre-filter that strips byte-identical copies and as the final verify primitive. The fuzzy detector is the revisited SimHash, not MinHash plus LSH, because the hardened one-hundred-byte memory ceiling killed MinHash's fit while leaving SimHash comfortably inside it. SimHash's recall weakness, which is exactly why I stashed it earlier, is fixed by widening to a 128-bit fingerprint and a four-block pigeonhole band, lifting first-stage recall to about ninety-two percent, then a cheap on-demand verify recovers precision. So the loser-by-accuracy became the winner-by-constraint once the binding constraint changed. That is the decision.
units.thought: Decision: exact hashing as pre-filter and verify, with a revisited 128-bit simhash plus four-block banding as the fuzzy detector, because the hardened memory ceiling killed minhash plus lsh while simhash now fits.

units.full: Now lock the parameters into a concrete spec. Tokenization: five-word shingles, lowercased and whitespace-normalized, with stopword-aware feature weighting so boilerplate does not dominate the fingerprint. Fingerprint: 128-bit SimHash over the weighted shingle features. Banding: four blocks of thirty-two bits, each prefix-indexed in its own sorted table, catching pairs within roughly sixteen bits of Hamming distance, which corresponds to the eighty-percent similarity target. Verification: on each candidate, an exact normalized-hash equality check first, then a full 128-bit Hamming recheck, accepting at distance threshold sixteen. Memory: sixteen-byte fingerprint plus sixty-four bytes of index equals eighty bytes per document, eighty gigabytes at a billion documents, inside the hundred-gigabyte ceiling.
units.thought: Locked spec: 5-word weighted shingles, 128-bit simhash, four 32-bit banding blocks for within-16-bit pairs, exact-hash plus Hamming verify, at 80 bytes per document inside the 100 GB ceiling.

units.full: Final verification pass against the original requirements. Recall: about ninety-two percent first-stage from the band, and verification only prunes false positives so it does not cost recall, clearing the ninety-percent target. Precision: the exact-hash and full-Hamming verify on the small candidate set drives false positives down hard, so precision is high. Throughput: 128-bit fingerprint computation plus four prefix lookups plus a handful of verifies is a few microseconds per document, holding fifty thousand per second across cores. Memory: eighty bytes per document, eighty gigabytes at a billion, under the hundred-gigabyte ceiling with headroom for growth. Every constraint I pinned at the start is met. The design is complete and the parameters are justified, not guessed.
units.thought: Final check passes: about 92 percent recall, high precision from the verify stage, 50k docs/sec throughput, and 80 bytes per document under the 100 GB ceiling, so every pinned constraint is met.

units.full: Unrelated administrative thought now that the design is settled: I should book a slot with the platform team to walk through the index rollout, and I keep meaning to reply to the recruiting email about the conference talk next month. Also the office coffee machine has been broken for a week and somebody should actually file the ticket rather than everyone grumbling at it. None of this touches the algorithm; it is just the mental residue that surfaces the moment a hard problem releases its grip on my attention. Let me note it and get back to writing up the design document properly.
units.thought: Unrelated aside about booking a platform rollout meeting, replying to a recruiting email, and the broken office coffee machine, none of which touch the algorithm.

units.full: One more recent observation before I write up: someone on the team asked whether we should add a language-detection step so we do not compare English documents against Spanish ones, which would waste candidate slots. It is a reasonable productionization idea and probably worth a cheap language tag as a partition key on the band tables. But it is orthogonal to the core near-duplicate algorithm I just settled, an optimization layered on top rather than a change to the SimHash banding and verify design. I will note it as future work in the spec rather than reopen the converged design, which is finished. The core algorithm decision stands.
units.thought: Recent observation: a language-detection partition key is a reasonable future optimization layered on top, but orthogonal to the settled simhash banding and verify design, so it is noted as future work, not a reopen.

units.full: Pulling the whole thing together into the final design statement for the document. Near-duplicate detection at a billion documents proceeds in two tiers behind an exact-hash pre-filter. Tier one is a 128-bit SimHash over five-word weighted shingles, indexed by four thirty-two-bit banding blocks in sorted prefix tables, which surfaces candidates for any pair within about sixteen bits of Hamming distance, the eighty-percent-similarity operating point, at roughly ninety-two percent recall. Tier two verifies each candidate with exact-hash equality then a full 128-bit Hamming check at threshold sixteen, recovering precision. Total footprint is eighty bytes per document, eighty gigabytes at a billion, throughput fifty thousand per second. MinHash plus LSH was stronger on raw recall but did not fit the hardened memory ceiling, so SimHash, revisited under constraint, is the chosen core.
units.thought: Final design: exact-hash pre-filter, a 128-bit simhash with four-block banding as tier-one candidate generator near 92 percent recall, then a Hamming verify tier, at 80 bytes per document, minhash plus lsh rejected on memory.

units.full: Let me pressure-test throughput more carefully, because fifty thousand documents per second is a hard line and I have been hand-waving the constant factors. Computing a 128-bit SimHash means hashing every shingle feature, and a long document might have a thousand shingles, so that is a thousand hash evaluations and a thousand weighted bit-accumulations per document. At a couple of nanoseconds per hash that is a few microseconds just for the fingerprint, before any band lookup. Across sixteen cores that is plenty for fifty thousand per second, but only if I keep the per-shingle work tight and avoid allocation in the hot loop. So the fingerprint stage, not the band lookup, is the throughput bottleneck, and it is feature-count bound.
units.thought: Throughput compute: a 128-bit simhash on a thousand-shingle document is a few microseconds of feature hashing, so the fingerprint stage, not band lookup, is the bottleneck, holding 50k per second across sixteen cores.

units.full: I want to verify precision more rigorously than waving at the verify stage, because false positives waste downstream work even if they are eventually removed. The band surfaces any pair within sixteen bits, and random unrelated documents differ by about sixty-four bits on a 128-bit fingerprint, so a chance collision within sixteen bits is astronomically rare; the cumulative binomial tail of sixty-four-or-fewer agreeing bits landing under sixteen disagreements is negligible. So nearly every banded candidate is a genuine near-neighbor, and the exact-hash plus full-Hamming verify mostly confirms rather than rejects. Precision out of the band alone is already high, well above ninety-five percent, and the verify stage pushes it toward certainty. The two-tier design is precision-safe, not just recall-safe.
units.thought: Precision verify: unrelated 128-bit fingerprints differ by about 64 bits, so a within-16-bit chance collision is negligible, making band precision already above 95 percent before the exact-plus-Hamming verify confirms it.

units.full: Coming back to the main line, I realize I should re-examine one thing I dismissed: whether a stripped-down MinHash could serve as the on-demand verify signal rather than a plain Hamming recheck, since verification is computed lazily on a tiny candidate set and never stored at rest. The earlier memory objection to MinHash was about storing full signatures for a billion documents; but if I only compute a short MinHash, say thirty-two hashes, on the handful of survivors at query time, the storage cost is zero and I get a direct Jaccard estimate that is more faithful to set overlap than Hamming distance on a compressed fingerprint. So the idea I rejected for the index might be perfect for the verify tier. Worth revisiting.
units.thought: Revisiting minhash for the verify tier: computing a short 32-hash signature lazily on the tiny candidate set costs zero storage and gives a faithful Jaccard estimate, so the index-rejected idea fits verification.

units.full: Deciding on the verify-tier signal. A lazy thirty-two-hash MinHash on each candidate gives a Jaccard estimate with about eighteen percent standard error, which is coarse but fine as a confirm-or-reject gate around the eighty-percent line when paired with the exact-hash equality shortcut for true copies. It is strictly more faithful to content overlap than reusing the SimHash Hamming distance, which can be fooled by feature-weight quirks. The cost is a few dozen extra hash evaluations per candidate, and candidates are low-tens per query, so it is negligible against the fingerprint stage. So the verify tier becomes: exact-hash equality first, then a lazy thirty-two-hash MinHash Jaccard check, then accept. MinHash earns a place after all, just not in the index.
units.thought: Decision: verify tier is exact-hash equality, then a lazy 32-hash minhash Jaccard check, which is more faithful than reusing simhash Hamming distance and negligible in cost; minhash returns at verify, not in the index.

units.full: I need to plan the parallelization and the build path, since ingest at this scale is a distributed job. Fingerprint computation is embarrassingly parallel, one document at a time with no shared state, so I shard the corpus across workers and each emits 128-bit fingerprints into a fingerprint store. The four banding tables are built by sorting fingerprints on each block prefix, which is a distributed sort, four passes over the fingerprint store, each producing one sorted prefix table. Query-time near-neighbor lookup then probes all four tables and unions the candidate sets. Incremental inserts append to a small unsorted overflow region per table, merged into the sorted tables on a periodic compaction, the usual log-structured-merge pattern. So build is parallel sort, query is four prefix probes, inserts are buffered.
units.thought: Parallelization plan: fingerprinting is embarrassingly parallel, the four banding tables are built by distributed prefix sorts, queries probe all four, and incremental inserts buffer in an overflow region merged by periodic compaction.

units.full: Verifying that incremental inserts do not silently degrade recall, because an unmerged overflow region that the query path forgets to probe would miss recent near-duplicates entirely. So the query must probe both the sorted prefix tables and the per-table overflow buffer, and the overflow buffer must be small enough to scan linearly without blowing the per-query latency budget. If compaction runs often enough to keep each overflow under, say, a few thousand entries, a linear scan of four small buffers adds only microseconds, and recall against the freshest documents stays intact. The failure mode to guard against is an overflow that grows unbounded between compactions, so compaction cadence is a real operational parameter, not an afterthought. With that guard, incremental recall holds.
units.thought: Verifying incremental recall: the query must scan both sorted tables and the small overflow buffer, so compaction cadence bounding each overflow to a few thousand entries keeps fresh-document recall intact within latency.

units.full: Brief aside that just occurred to me and is genuinely off-topic: I never confirmed whether the cafeteria does the taco thing on Thursdays or Fridays, and I told a colleague I would check before lunch. Also my phone has been buzzing with calendar reminders for a dentist appointment I keep deferring, and I should just book it for real this time. And the parking garage is doing maintenance next week so I need to find an alternative. These have nothing to do with fingerprints or banding or recall; they are pure interrupt noise surfacing now that the hard design work is essentially done. Noted, ignored, back to the work.
units.thought: Off-topic aside about cafeteria taco day, a deferred dentist appointment, and parking garage maintenance next week, none of which touch fingerprints, banding, or recall.

units.full: Final convergence and sign-off on the whole design. The near-duplicate detector is settled: an exact-hash pre-filter, then a 128-bit SimHash over five-word weighted shingles indexed by four thirty-two-bit banding blocks for within-sixteen-bit Hamming candidate generation at about ninety-two percent recall, then a verify tier of exact-hash equality plus a lazy thirty-two-hash MinHash Jaccard check, all at eighty bytes per document and fifty thousand documents per second, built by distributed prefix sort with log-structured incremental inserts. MinHash plus LSH lost the index slot to the memory ceiling but won the verify slot; SimHash, the idea I rejected then revisited under constraint, anchors the index. Every original requirement, recall, precision, throughput, and memory, is met and justified. I am confident; the design is done.
units.thought: Final sign-off: exact-hash pre-filter, 128-bit simhash banding index near 92 percent recall, and an exact-plus-lazy-minhash verify tier, at 80 bytes per document and 50k per second, with every requirement met.

probes.why: Focusing on the locked SimHash parameter spec (#21, b7) should pull back the SimHash revisit #12 (b6, 9 ticks earlier, shares simhash/banding/fingerprint/memory) and the band-widening compute #14 (b6, 7 ticks earlier, shares simhash/banding/recall), both on another branch, and must NOT surface the immediately-prior reproducibility aside #15 (seed/coffee), which shares no design terms.

Focusing on the final verification pass (#22, b7) should surface the old LSH recall verify #8 (b2, 14 ticks earlier, shares recall/precision/verification/banding) and the SimHash revisit #12 (b6, 10 ticks earlier, shares simhash/banding/memory), both other-branch, not the recent off-topic reproducibility aside #15.

Focusing on the final design statement (#25, b7, simhash/banding/hamming/verification) should pull the SimHash band compute #14 (b6, 11 ticks earlier, shares simhash/hamming/banding/recall) and the two-tier recall verify #17 (b6, 8 ticks earlier, shares simhash/hamming/banding), not the immediately-recent admin aside #23 (coffee/email/meeting).

Focusing on the merge-back decision (#20, simhash/minhash/lsh/banding/memory) should surface the earlier take-stock #9 (11 ticks earlier, shares minhash/lsh/simhash/memory/tradeoff) and the memory-constraint observation #10 (10 ticks earlier, shares minhash/lsh/memory/budget) that forced it, not the off-branch reproducibility aside #15.

Focusing on the final sign-off (#33, simhash/minhash/lsh/banding/verification/recall) should surface the earlier merge-back decision #20 (13 ticks earlier, same entities) and the final design statement #25 (8 ticks earlier, b7 other-branch, shares simhash/minhash/lsh/banding/verification), not the long-ago off-topic aside #15.
